meta.title: Cadence Metronome Guides
meta.description: Short guides to get the most out of Cadence Metronome: tap tempo, accents, and subdivisions.

index.title: Cadence Metronome Guides
index.description: Short, practical guides to get the most out of the metronome, from tap tempo to subdivisions.

tap-tempo.title: Tap Tempo: find the tempo by tapping
tap-tempo.summary: When you know how it sounds but not the number, tap the beat and let Cadence Metronome work out the BPM.

sections.heading: What it is
body: Tap Tempo lets you set the tempo by tapping a button in time with the music, instead of guessing a number. The app measures the time between your taps and turns it into BPM (beats per minute).

sections.heading: Step by step
steps: Open Cadence Metronome and find the Tap Tempo button.
Tap the button in time with the beat you want, one tap per pulse. Two taps already give you a first reading.
Keep tapping a few more beats: the app averages your taps to refine the tempo and smooth out small inconsistencies.
When the number settles, stop tapping. That's your tempo, ready to use.

sections.heading: Tips
list: Tap quarter notes (the main pulse), not subdivisions, so the BPM comes out right.
Four to eight taps are enough for a stable reading; more taps, more accuracy.
If you slip, pause for a moment and start again: the app resets the average.
You can fine-tune the value afterward with the BPM dial, from 20 to 300.

accents.title: Accents and time signatures: shape the pulse
accents.summary: The time signature groups the beats; accents mark which ones land harder. Together they turn a flat click into a rhythm with direction.

sections.heading: The time signature
body: The time signature sets how many beats are in each cycle and how they group. 4/4 has four beats; 3/4 has three (the waltz); 6/8 has six grouped in threes. Pick the one that matches the piece you're practicing.

sections.heading: The accents
body: By default, the first beat of each bar is accented, louder: it's what orients you within the cycle. In Cadence Metronome you can customize which beats are accented, tapping each beat to mark it or clear its accent.

sections.heading: Step by step
steps: Choose the time signature (4/4, for example) in the meter settings.
Look at the row of beats: the first one shows as accented.
Tap any beat to accent it; tap it again to make it normal.
Hit play and hear how the accent pattern gives the bar its shape.

sections.heading: What it's for
list: Feeling the "one" of each bar without counting.
Practicing compound or odd meters by marking the accents where they belong.
Building your own accent patterns for specific exercises.

subdivisions.title: Subdivisions: practice between the beats
subdivisions.summary: Splitting the beat into equal parts —eighths, triplets, sixteenths— helps you play accurately and stop rushing or dragging.

sections.heading: What a subdivision is
body: A subdivision splits each beat into smaller equal parts. If the beat is a quarter note, eighths divide it in two, triplets in three, and sixteenths in four. Hearing those parts gives you a much finer internal grid.

sections.heading: The available subdivisions
list: Eighth notes — two hits per beat.
Sixteenth notes — four hits per beat.

sections.heading: Step by step
steps: Set a comfortable tempo with the dial or with Tap Tempo.
Choose the subdivision: eighths, triplets, or sixteenths.
Hit play: you'll hear the main pulse plus the in-between hits, on a softer sound.
Play your part leaning on that grid; raise the tempo gradually once it feels solid.

sections.heading: Tips
list: Start slow: the subdivision exposes rushing and dragging you can't hear at tempo.
Use it for passages with eighths or triplets that are hard to keep even.
Once it's internalized, drop the subdivision and hold the precision on the pulse alone.
